mcqs.question: In a system with 100 frames and 5 processes of equal size using equal allocation, how many frames does each process receive?
mcqs.explanation: Equal allocation divides total frames equally among processes: 100 frames / 5 processes = 20 frames per process.

mcqs.question: Which frame allocation strategy gives more frames to larger processes?
mcqs.explanation: Proportional allocation distributes frames based on process size, so larger processes receive proportionally more frames.

mcqs.question: What is thrashing primarily caused by?
mcqs.explanation: Thrashing occurs when processes do not have enough frames, causing excessive page faults and spending more time paging than executing.

mcqs.question: In which type of allocation can one process take frames from another process?
mcqs.explanation: Global allocation allows all processes to compete for any available frame, so a process gaining frames affects the availability for other processes.

mcqs.question: The working set model attempts to keep which of the following in memory?

options.text: Only the pages currently being referenced
Pages that will be used in the future

mcqs.explanation: The working set model maintains the pages a process has used recently (defined by a window), attempting to keep active pages in memory to reduce page faults.

mcqs.question: Which technique monitors page fault frequency to adjust frame allocation?
mcqs.explanation: Page Fault Frequency (PFF) is a technique where the OS monitors how often a process incurs page faults and adjusts frame allocation accordingly to maintain optimal performance.

mcqs.question: What is the minimum number of frames determined by?

options.text: Maximum pages referenced by a single instruction

mcqs.explanation: The minimum number of frames is determined by the maximum number of distinct pages any single instruction can reference, to prevent multiple page faults during instruction execution.

mcqs.question: Which allocation policy ensures predictable performance for individual processes?
mcqs.explanation: Local allocation with local replacement ensures each process is limited to its own frames, providing predictable performance independent of other processes' behavior.

mcqs.question: As the degree of multiprogramming increases, what happens to the average frames per process?
mcqs.explanation: With fixed total frames, as more processes share memory (higher multiprogramming), each process receives fewer frames on average, potentially leading to more page faults.

mcqs.question: Which is NOT a frame allocation approach?
mcqs.explanation: Circular allocation is not a standard frame allocation approach. The main approaches are equal, proportional, and priority-based allocation.

mcqs.question: In priority-based allocation, which processes typically receive fewer frames?
mcqs.explanation: In priority-based allocation, lower priority processes receive fewer frames compared to higher priority processes, ensuring critical processes have sufficient memory.

mcqs.question: What happens when PFF is too low during page fault frequency control?

options.text: Allocate more frames to the process
Reclaim excess frames from the process

mcqs.explanation: If PFF is too low, it indicates the process has more frames than needed. The OS reclaims excess frames to allocate to processes that need them more.

mcqs.question: Local page replacement allows a process to replace pages from:
mcqs.explanation: Local page replacement restricts a process to replacing only pages within its own allocated frames, not affecting other processes' frames.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a symptom of insufficient frame allocation?
mcqs.explanation: Thrashing is a clear symptom of insufficient frame allocation, where processes spend more time in page I/O than actual execution.

mcqs.question: The allocation of frames in a paging system is analogous to which of the following in real life?

options.text: Assigning parking slots in a mall

mcqs.back: Lockers (frames) are fixed-size containers used to store items (pages) belonging to different individuals (processes), similar to how frames store pages.

mcqs.question: What is the main disadvantage of equal allocation?

options.text: It is too complex to implement
It does not consider process size or priority

mcqs.explanation: Equal allocation gives the same frames to all processes regardless of their actual needs, which can be inefficient as small processes waste frames while large processes suffer page faults.

mcqs.question: Which algorithm is used in the working set model to determine frame requirements?

options.text: Moving window to track page references

mcqs.explanation: The working set model uses a moving window to track which pages have been referenced within a specific time period to determine the working set size and required frames.

mcqs.question: Global replacement combined with local allocation means:

options.text: A process can use any frame but only replace its own pages
A process can use any frame and replace any page
A process is limited to its frames but can replace any page in them

mcqs.explanation: With local allocation combined with global replacement, each process has a fixed set of frames, but within those frames, it can replace any page (whether its own or not, as frames are dedicated).

mcqs.question: What is the relationship between available free frames and frame allocation?

options.text: Free frames are never used in allocation
Free frames are equally distributed in global allocation
Free frames serve as a pool for global allocation decisions

mcqs.explanation: In global allocation, free frames serve as a common pool from which any process can be allocated frames based on need and current system state.

mcqs.question: A process with a high locality of reference needs:

options.text: More frames to contain its working set
Fewer frames because it references few pages
Frames only in the swap area

mcqs.explanation: A process with high locality references only a small set of pages repeatedly, so it needs fewer frames to contain its working set and maintain low page fault rates.
